coredata.dc:title: Evaluating the catalytic potential of Lithium-decorated graphene quantum dots for small molecule activation

coredata.dc:description: The search for effective catalysts in small molecule activation has intensified as industries seek efficient and cost-effective solutions. Lithium (Li), known for its unique electronic properties, is of significant interest as a catalyst. However, its specific catalytic potential when anchored on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has not been fully explored. This study investigates the catalytic potential of lithium (Li) adatoms on GQDs with various edge conformations (zigzag and armchair) and sizes (24 and 42 atoms). Using density functional theory (DFT), we examine the interactions of Li-decorated GQDs with small molecules such as H2, N2, CO, O2, and CO2. Our findings reveal that Li-GQD complexes exhibit optimal catalytic activity for all these molecules, based on binding energy, charge distribution, and bond length changes. The smallest GQD, coronene (24 carbon atoms), shows the most promising catalytic activity, providing experimental leads for synthesizing and testing efficient Li-anchored catalysts.

head.ce:title: Evaluating the catalytic potential of Lithium-decorated graphene quantum dots for small molecule activation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The search for effective catalysts in small molecule activation has intensified as industries seek efficient and cost-effective solutions. Lithium (Li), known for its unique electronic properties, is of significant interest as a catalyst. However, its specific catalytic potential when anchored on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has not been fully explored. This study investigates the catalytic potential of lithium (Li) adatoms on GQDs with various edge conformations (zigzag and armchair) and sizes (24 and 42 atoms). Using density functional theory (DFT), we examine the interactions of Li-decorated GQDs with small molecules such as H2, N2, CO, O2, and CO2. Our findings reveal that Li-GQD complexes exhibit optimal catalytic activity for all these molecules, based on binding energy, charge distribution, and bond length changes. The smallest GQD, coronene (24 carbon atoms), shows the most promising catalytic activity, providing experimental leads for synthesizing and testing efficient Li-anchored catalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The activation of small linear molecules such as dioxygen (O2), dihydrogen (H2), dinitrogen (N2), carbon dioxide (CO2), and carbon monoxide (CO) is a fundamental challenge in chemistry, with significant implications for energy conversion, environmental remediation, and industrial synthesis. [1–5] These molecules, despite their abundance, are generally inert, pose a challenge for their direct utilisation in chemical transformations. Therefore, effective activation is crucial for converting these molecules into valuable chemicals and fuels, which can significantly impact various fields. [6] For instance, the Nitrogen Reduction Reaction (NRR), Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR), and Carbon dioxide Reduction Reaction (CO2RR) are fundamental to developing sustainable energy solutions. [7–11] Understanding and improving the mechanisms for activating these small molecules can lead to substantial advancements in these areas.

Traditionally, transition metal complexes have played a dominant role in the activation of these small molecules due to their ability to facilitate bond cleavage through unique electronic properties. [12–16] However, these materials often suffer from issues such as sustainability on account of their multiple oxidation states. [17] Therefore, research has begun to explore alternative strategies that move away from transition metals, driven by the need for more sustainable and cost-effective solutions. In this context, main-group complexes have garnered significant attention. [18,19] Among the alternatives, carbon-based materials, particularly graphene, have shown considerable potential in activation of small molecules. [20–25] Graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice, possesses remarkable electronic, thermal, and mechanical properties that make it better candidate for catalytic applications. [26–31] Besides graphene quantum dots (GQDs), [32,33] graphene nanoribbons (GNRs), [34–37] which are narrower strips of graphene, also exhibit unique electronic properties and potential, over graphene, for catalytic applications, [38] yet remain less explored for the activation of small molecules. [39]

Particularly, Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), [40,41] nanoscale fragments of graphene, exhibit exceptional electronic and optical characteristics due to quantum confinement effects, which are size-dependent and significantly enhance their reactivity and catalytic performance. [42–46] The edge termination of GQDs, whether saturated with hydrogen, oxygen, or other functional groups, influence their electronic structure and chemical reactivity. [47–51] Additionally, the conformation of the edges: zigzag or armchair edges affects their properties, with zigzag edges typically showing metallic behaviour and armchair edges displaying semiconducting properties. [52–56] Recent developments in the synthesis of graphene quantum dots have significantly improved their practical advantages, including ease of production, cost-effectiveness, and stability, thereby broadening their potential applications in various fields. [57,58] The catalytic potential of GQDs can be further enhanced by anchoring metal atoms onto their surface, introducing new active sites and modifying their electronic structure. [59–63] Researchers have explored lithium, magnesium, and titanium- decorated GQDs for different applications, including energy storage, sensors, and catalysis. Particularly, lithium forms highly reactive bonds with the π-electron system of GQDs, enhancing the durability and longevity of the GQDs in various catalytic processes. [64–73] GQDs high surface area and tunable electronic properties make them better candidates for facilitating small molecule activation. [74,75] This modification strategy increases the reactivity of GQDs, providing a versatile platform for designing catalysts tailored to specific reactions.

However, a clear understanding on the exact potential of Li decorated quantum dots on small molecule activation is not well understood. Few spare studies have evaluated the potential of Li decorated quantum dots for hydrogen storage. [76,77] Hence, to have a more clear and exact understanding on the catalytic potential of Li decorated quantum dots, in the present work, using Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations, we explore the adsorption and activation mechanism of various small molecules on Li-decorated GQDs as a function of edge effects and (zigzag and armchair) and size effects (24 and 42 atoms). Our findings provide insights into the electronic interaction between the adsorbed molecules and the Li-decorated GQDs, revealing the potential of Li-GQDs as efficient catalysts for small molecule activation.

All calculations are performed within the framework of Density Functional Theory (DFT), [78] employing a linear combination of Gaussian orbitals as implemented in the deMon2k code. [79] Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are generated and optimized using the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange and correlation functional with double zeta-valence polarization (DZVP) as the basis set for the valence electrons. [70–82] The GEN-A2 auxiliary functions are used to fit the charge density. The convergence of the geometries relies on gradient and displacement criteria with a threshold of 10−5 au for geometry optimization and 10−7 au for the convergence of SCF cycles. Various sizes (24 and 42 atoms) and edge conformations (zigzag and armchair) of GQDs are considered in the study. The optimized conformations for all GQD systems are shown in Fig. 1 . The nomenclature used refers to the number of carbon atoms and edge type, where ‘zz’ indicates a zigzag edge and ‘arc’ denotes an armchair edge: (a) 24-GQD-zz, (b) 24-GQD-arc, (c) 42-GQD-zz, and (d) 42-GQD-arc. For all GQD systems, the lowest spin states are used: singlet states for systems with an even number of electrons and doublet states for systems with an odd number of electrons. The Restricted Kohn Sham (RKS) formalism is employed for singlet spin states, and the Restricted Open Kohn Sham (ROKS) formalism is used for doublet spin states. The dispersion correction for the DFT method, [83], is applied using the deMon2k code, [84], to account for London dispersion forces. [85] For the Li-decorated GQDs, a comparative electronic structure analysis is performed using Bader, Mulliken, and Löwdin population analysis to elucidate the interaction between the lithium (Li) atom and the GQDs. [86,87] Charge transfer between the Li atom and the adsorbed linear molecules is assessed through Bader population analysis as implemented in the deMon2k software package.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adatom anchoring of Li on GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to form a stable catalyst, we have anchored lithium (Li) atoms onto graphene quantum dot (GQD) surfaces with both zigzag and armchair edge conformations, as depicted in Fig. 2 . The binding energy of Li with these surfaces is shown in Fig. 3 , illustrating that lithium binds exothermically to GQD surfaces in all cases, with negative binding energies, indicating a thermodynamically favourable interaction. The average bond lengths of the Li atom with 24-GQD-zz, 24-GQD-arc, 42-GQD-zz, and 42-GQD-arc are 2.28 Å, 2.24 Å, 2.25 Å, and 2.24 Å, respectively. For the electronic structure analysis, we evaluated the charges on various atoms using three population analysis methods: Bader, Löwdin, and Mulliken. Employing these methods allowed us to thoroughly validate the charge distribution and interaction characteristics between the Li atom and the GQD surfaces. Each method offers a different perspective on the electron density distribution and charge transfer, thus ensuring a thorough understanding of the electronic properties of the system. Notably, in this case, all three methods yielded consistent trends in the charge analysis, confirming the robustness and reliability of our findings. Irrespective of the edge conformation and size of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the lithium (Li) atom consistently carries a positive charge, while an equal amount of negative charge is distributed among the GQD atoms, including the passivated hydrogen atoms. The comparative charge on Li atom for each system is presented in Fig. 4 , which demonstrates these consistent trends across the different methods.

Further, we evaluated the catalytic performance of Li-GQDs complexes toward industrially relevant gas molecules (viz., dihydrogen (H2), dinitrogen (N2), carbon monoxide (CO), dioxygen (O2), and carbon dioxide (CO2)) on Li-GQDs complexes. Two distinctive adsorption modes are considered for the gas molecules viz., side-on (s) and end-on (e) orientations. An optimal binding energy is crucial for effective catalysis; if the binding energy is too weak, the molecules may desorb before meaningful reactions occurs, while excessively strong binding energy can impede the release of reaction products or prevent effective interaction with other reactants. Thus, an intermediate binding energy range is essential, allowing for a stable association with the catalyst and facilitating reactant participation in chemical reactions at reasonable rates. [88] Achieving this balance is critical in designing efficient catalytic systems. Furthermore, the activation of gas molecules should be evident through structural changes such as bond lengths elongation or change in bond angles. These structural changes in the gas molecule is critical for the successive reaction steps of the reaction mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activation of H2 molecule on Li-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized geometries for the interaction of H2 molecule with Li-GQDs complexes are shown in Supplementary Table S.T.1. Their corresponding adsorption energies are given in Fig. 5 . The adsorption energy values reveal that H2 binds to the Li-GQDs complexes exothermically through both side-on (s) and end-on (e) orientations. In case of both sizes, GQDs with zigzag edges are seen to demonstrate more exothermic adsorption for hydrogen molecule, particularly in a side-on orientation. The adsorption energy of the hydrogen molecule is highest on the 42 atom GQD with zigzag edge. The adsorption energy values on the zig-zag edges in either orientation is representative of chemical adsorption of hydrogen molecule. The adsorption energies on the arm chair edges on the other hand fall in the quasi-molecular adsorption range which is noted to be between −0.2 eV to −1 eV. [89]

Electronic structure analysis of adsorbed complexes is carried out next. To begin with, we analyse the variations in the HH bond length on adsorption to Li-GQD complex. There is a small increase in the HH bond length in all cases from 0.74 Å to 0.76–0.77 Å. This increase in the bond length is accompanied by the red shift in the HH bond stretching frequency as shown in the table. The red shift is highest for the case of 24 atom GQD, the higher red shifts are inherently correlated to higher activation of the molecular bond. Next, Bader population analysis is evaluated for all the complexes and is given in Table 1 . Prior to H2 adsorption, the GQD surface carries a cumulative negative charge, while the Li atom acts as a positive center (refer to the values in red in Table 1). On adsorption of hydrogen molecule, there is a very small charge between the Li-GQD complex and the hydrogen molecule. This is a consistent trend noted for all complexes.

In conclusion, the ideal binding strength for hydrogen storage lies between physisorption and chemisorption, with binding energies ranging from −0.1 to −0.8 eV, making it well-suited for storage at ambient pressures and temperatures. [89] Therefore, the armchair conformations of both 24 and 42 atom GQDs are promising materials for hydrogen storage. On the other hand, the zigzag conformations exhibit strong catalytic activity toward H2 molecule, as understood from the higher binding energy and greater red shift in a HH bond IR stretching frequencies. These are the better suitable energy compared to bigger size Li and Na atom decorated GQD, the H2 molecule is physisorb on the GQD system, which makes it a good candidate for reversible hydrogen storage. [90,91]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activation of N2 molecule on Li-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized geometries of N2 molecule adsorbed on Li-GQD complexes are shown in Supplementary Table 2. Fig. 6 illustrates the adsorption energies for these interactions, showing that N₂ binds to the Li-GQD complexes through energetically favourable exothermic processes in both side-on (s) and end-on (e) orientations. These results suggest that the zigzag edge configuration (zz) is more effective for N2 adsorption, particularly in the Li-42-GQD-zz structure, where both orientations provide robust binding and Li-24-GQD-zz structure demonstrates the intermediate binding energy of N2 with complex. Indicating that edge structure and molecular orientation play crucial roles in optimizing N2 adsorption on GQDs. The role of edge effect on the adsorption energy of the N2 molecule is lower for the case of 42 atom GQD in comparison with 24 atom GQD.

To gain deeper insights into the adsorption and activation mechanism of N2 molecule, an electronic structure analysis is carried out. Table 2 shows NN bond lengths of the adsorbed N2 molecule, distances between the Li atom and the N2 molecule, and red shift in the IR stretching frequency of dinitrogen molecule. There is a marginal increase in the NN bond length on adsorption onto Li atom anchored on GQDs. The bond length increases from 1.12 Å to 1.13 Å to 1.16 Å. The increase is higher on the 24‑carbon atom GQDs (1.16 Å). This increase in bond length is accompanied by a significant shift in the IR stretching frequency of the NN bond. The maximum increase is once again noted on the 24 atom GQD complex. The quantum size effect in our Li-24-GQD-zz complex leads to significantly improved catalytic performance compared to earlier reports, where lithium decorated pristine graphene surfaces showed less activation of N2 molecule. [92] In short, there is a weakening of the NN bond, a key factor in the dinitrogen activation process. With the highest red shift, 471 cm−1, Li-24-GQD-zz conformation is showing the most effective activation of N2 molecule with side-on orientation, with bond elongation from 1.12 Å to 1.16 Å. Bader population analysis is used to study the charge transfer during adsorption. Before N2 adsorption, the GQD surface holds a cumulative negative charge, while the Li atom serves as a positive center. For instance, in the Li-GQDs complexes, the total charge on the GQD surface (including terminal H atoms) is approximately −0.9 e, balanced by an equivalent positive charge on the Li adatom. Upon N2 adsorption, significant charge redistribution occurs across all Li-GQD complexes, with electrons transferring between the GQD, the Li atom, and the adsorbed N₂ molecule in both orientations. This charge movement, refer columns 6, 7, and 8 of Table 2, is indicative of effective molecular activation.

In conclusion, the zigzag edge conformations of Li-GQDs demonstrate strong catalytic activity toward the N2 molecule, as evidenced by the higher binding energy, significant bond elongation, and notable red shift in the NN bond IR stretching frequency, particularly in the Li-24-GQD-zz complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activation of CO molecule on Li-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized geometries of CO adsorbed on Li-GQD complexes are shown in Supplementary Table 3. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , the adsorption energies associated with these interactions reveal that CO binds strongly to the Li-GQD complexes, through the exothermic processes, with both side-on (s) and end-on (e) orientations being energetically favourable. Zigzag edges show higher binding energy compared to armchair edge conformations. For CO molecules, the end-on orientation is more favourable on smaller GQDs. In case of 42 atom GQD, the binding energy difference between end-on and side-on orientations decrease. This highlights the effects of size, edge conformation, and orientation of CO molecules on the surfaces.

Table 3 , provides detailed structural parameters, including the CO bond lengths, distances between the Li atom and the CO molecule, and observed red shift in the IR stretching frequency of CO molecule. These parameters are critical for understanding the extent of CO activation and the accompanying structural changes. Li-24-GQD with zigzag edge conformation showing the highest activation of molecule in side-on orientation with 415 cm−1 red shift and 1.2 Å bond length, followed by this other complexes also shown the slight activation of CO molecule, see Table 3. Upon adsorption onto Li-anchored GQDs, the CO molecule shows a slight increase in bond length, ranging from 1.15 Å to 1.16–1.20 Å. Again, the Li atom anchored to the 24-atom GQD shows greater bond length elongation, from 1.15 Å to 1.20 Å, with a side-on orientation in both zigzag and armchair edge conformations. To unravel the electronic characteristics of CO adsorption, Bader population analysis is carried out, focusing on charge redistribution during the process. Initially, the GQD surface exhibits a negative charge, while the Li atom maintains a positive charge. For instance, in the Li-GQDs complexes, prior to CO adsorption, the GQD holds a charge of approximately −0.9 e, with the Li adatom balancing this with a corresponding positive charge. Upon adsorption of the CO molecule, there is a noticeable shift in electron density across the Li-GQD complexes, leading to a significant charge redistribution. This electron movement, refer columns 6, 7, and 8 of Table 3, between the GQD, Li atom, and CO molecule in Li-24-GQD complex is more as compare to Li-42-GQD complex suggests that the effective activation of CO on Li-24-GQD complex.

In conclusion, the Li-24-GQD-zz complex, which demonstrated the best activation for H2 and N2, similarly exhibits the highest catalytic performance for CO. This is indicated by significant bond elongation, a strong red shift in the CO bond IR stretching frequency, and notable charge redistribution, particularly in the side-on orientation. Researchers have performed a DFT investigation on the adsorption of CO molecules over Co-, Al-, and Ge-embedded graphene, and reported adsorption energy values comparable to those obtained in our study. [93,94]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activation of O2 molecule on Li-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized geometries of O2 molecule adsorbed on Li-GQD complexes are shown in Supplementary Table 4. As depicted in Fig. 8 , the adsorption energies show that O2 exhibits the highest binding of molecule with surface among the tested molecules, indicating a particularly stable and strong interaction with the GQD complex in both side-on and end-on orientations. The zigzag edge of the 42-GQD complex shows highest molecular adsorption energy. Followed by this other complexes also shows the strong binding of O2 with surfaces.

structural parameters such as OO bond lengths, distances between the Li atom and the O2 molecule, and bond red shift in the IR stretching frequency of O2 molecule are critical indicators of molecular activation, are shown in Table 4 . irrespective of higher adsorption energies of O2 molecule on Li-42-GQDs surface, Li-24-GQD surface shows the highest activation of O2 molecule in side-on orientation with 445 cm−1 bond red shift in IR stretching frequency which resulted in a higher bond elongation of O2 molecule i.e. 1.37 Å. In case of O2 molecule Table 4 clearly shows that molecular orientation plays a crucial role in activation, with higher bond elongation and bond red shift in the IR stretching frequency consistently occurring in the side-on orientation compare to end-on orientations. Next, Bader population analysis is evaluated for all the complexes and is given in Table 4. Prior adsorption of O2 molecule, the GQD surface (including passivated H atoms) for all the Li-GQDs complex carries a cumulative negative charge of −0.9 e, balanced by an equivalent positive charge on the Li atom. Upon adsorption of O2, there is a significant charge redistribution, reflecting a strong interaction and effective activation of the molecule. This charge transfer, refer columns 6, 7, and 8 of Table 4, involves electron movement among the GQD, the Li atom, and the O2 molecule.

In conclusion, despite the higher adsorption energy of O2 on the Li-42-GQD-zz surface, the Li-24-GQD-zz complex exhibits the most effective activation of the O₂ molecule, similar to its performance with H2, N2, and CO. This is evidenced by significant bond elongation, a strong red shift in the OO bond IR stretching frequency, and notable charge redistribution, particularly in the side-on orientation, highlighting the complex's superior activity for O2 activation. However, although it shows good activation of the O2 molecule, the binding of O₂ to the surface is too strong, which could lead to poor catalytic performance in experimental conditions, as excessively strong binding energy can hinder product release or block further interactions with reactants. This underscores the importance of an intermediate binding energy range for efficient catalysis. [88] Researchers conducted a DFT investigation on O2 molecule adsorption over Co-, Al-, and Ge-embedded graphene and obtained a slightly lower range of O2 adsorption energies than those found in our study. [93,94]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activation of CO2 molecule on Li-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized geometries of CO2 adsorbed on Li-GQD complexes are shown in Supplementary Table 5. As illustrated in Fig. 9 , the adsorption energies associated with these interactions reveal that CO2 binds strongly to the Li-GQD complexes through exothermic processes, with both side-on (s) and end-on (e) orientations being energetically favourable. From the adsorption energy plot, the orientation of the molecule shows a significant difference in its binding to the Li-GQD surfaces. In all cases, the end-on orientation of the molecule results in stronger binding with the surface than the side-on orientation. Among all the GQD surfaces, the 42-atom GQD with zigzag edge conformation exhibits the strongest interaction between the CO2 molecule and the surface in both orientations.

From the Table 5 , the bond lengths, bond angle and bond red shift in the IR stretching frequency of CO2 molecule reveal crucial insights into molecular activation. In end-on orientation Li decorated 42 atom GQD surface with armchair edge conformation reports a significant activation of CO2 molecule among all the complexes, for this complex the bond angle of the CO2 molecule is notably reduced from 180° to 128.84°, further activation of molecule is supported by change in bond length of molecule and bond red shift in the IR stretching frequency i.e. bond elongation is occurred between CO 1.18 Å to 1.26 Å with 740 cm−1 bond red shift in the IR stretching frequency. Similar activation of CO2 molecule is reported in end-on orientation of Li-24-GQDs of both zigzag and armchair edge conformation, see Table 5. This significant deformation suggests a strong interaction between the CO₂ molecule and the Li-GQD surfaces, indicating effective activation and interaction with the surface. Further, the Bader charge, analysis evaluated for all the complexes and is given in columns 7, 8 and 9 of Table 5, shows that significant electron transfer occurs between the GQD, Li atom, and the CO2 molecule during adsorption. The GQD surfaces and Li adatoms carry charges that vary depending on the orientation and specific GQD complex, leading to a polarization of the CO2 molecule. This charge redistribution is crucial for the activation process, as it weakens the bonds within the CO2 molecule, making it more amenable to catalytic reactions.

In conclusion, the activation of CO2 on Li-GQD surfaces is most pronounced on the Li-42-GQD-zz complex, especially in the end-on orientation, where the CO2 molecule undergoes significant structural deformation. This is marked by a notable reduction in bond angle, bond elongation, a substantial red shift in the IR stretching frequency, and extensive charge redistribution, all of which contribute to its effective activation. Similar activation is observed for the Li-24-GQD-zz complex, underscoring its catalytic efficiency for CO2 transformation. Our study demonstrates improved CO2 adsorption and activation compared to previous findings, where researchers examined CO2 activation on TM atom-decorated GQDs and P-doped graphene and Ti-doped GQD, but observed lower activation, as indicated by less CO bond weakening and minimal bond angle modification. [95–97]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of multiple Li atoms on GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the effects of multiple Li atoms on GQD interactions with small molecules, we focus on the 24-GQD-zz structure, which has been chosen due to its promising catalytic properties. This structure serves as an ideal case study for investigating how the presence of multiple Li atoms influences adsorption and interaction mechanisms between catalysts and small molecules. We have decorated two Li atoms at the interstitial site of the GQD surface, as shown in Fig. 10 . To assess their impact on the adsorption and activation of small molecules, we first adsorbed small molecules onto one of the Li atoms, see Supplementary Table 6. Furthermore, we increased the concentration of small molecules to investigate whether both Li atoms could simultaneously participate in activating two molecules optimized conformations are depicted in Supplementary Table 7.

Table 6 presents a comparison of adsorption energies (in eV) and bond lengths (in Å) for various small molecules adsorbed on both single and dual Li-decorated GQDs (2Li-24-GQD-zz). From the table, we observe that except for the H2 molecules, the adsorption energy is higher in the dual Li case compared to the single Li case. For example, the adsorption energy of H2 decreased from −1.34 eV (single Li) to −0.81 eV (dual Li), with the bond length remaining constant at 0.76 Å. In the case of N2, CO, and O2 higher adsorption energies were reported when two Li atoms are involved, suggesting that the dual Li configuration promotes the stronger interactions with the molecules, potentially indicating a higher capacity for molecule activation. In the case of CO2, a notable change in both the adsorption energy and bond angles is observed, with the energy shifting from −0.39 eV (single Li) to −1.98 eV (dual Li), accompanied by changes in bond angles shown in Table 6. These results suggest that the dual Li configuration significantly alters the adsorption behaviour, which may be critical for improving catalytic performance.

The above results demonstrate that graphene quantum dots (GQDs), an experimentally viable material, exhibit significant catalytic potential for small molecule activation. [57,58] Our study focused on the catalytic properties of lithium (Li) anchored on GQDs with various edge conformations and sizes. As discussed by Emil Roduner, optimal binding energy is crucial for effective catalysis. [86] In our analysis, for all the complexes oxygen (O2) molecules displayed notably high binding energy, indicating strong activation, this did not translate to ideal catalytic behaviour for further reactions. In contrast, for molecules like H2, N2, CO, and CO2, the combination of favourable binding energy, optimal charge distribution, and the resulting changes in bond length and bond angle make Li-anchored GQDs an ideal catalyst for these molecules activation. Additionally, these properties suggest that Li-GQDs could also serve as effective storage materials for these gas molecules.

The contrasting adsorption behaviour of O2, compared to other linear diatomic molecules, arises from its unique electronic structure. Unlike H2, N2, and CO, which exhibit stronger binding in the end-on orientation due to efficient orbital alignment, O2 prefers the side-on configuration. This can be attributed to its triplet ground state, where two degenerate π* anti-bonding orbitals symmetrically interact with the Li atom in the side-on orientation, resulting in strong adsorption energy, enhanced charge transfer, significant bond elongation (1.37 Å), and a pronounced red shift in the IR stretching frequency (445 cm−1). In contrast, the end-on configuration limits interaction to a single π* orbital, leading to weaker molecular activation. This highlights the critical role of orbital symmetry and electronic structure in governing adsorption trends.

The catalytic activity of the Li-decorated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is intricately linked to their electronic properties, specifically the work function and p-band center. As detailed in Table 7 , it is evident that the Li-24-GQD with a zigzag edge conformation demonstrates the most favourable electronic characteristics for catalytic activity, exhibiting the lowest work function and the most negative p-band center. This configuration suggests enhanced charge transfer capabilities, which are crucial for effective catalysis. Conversely, the Li-24-GQD with an armchair edge conformation and the Li-42-GQDs in both zigzag and armchair edge conformations show progressively higher work functions and less negative p-band centers, correlating with diminished catalytic efficiency. These electronic properties directly influence the catalytic mechanism by modulating charge transfer and adsorption strength. A lower work function facilitates charge transfer from the catalyst to the adsorbed molecules, enhancing activation, while a more negative p-band center strengthens interactions with key reaction intermediates. As a result, the Li-24-GQD-zz complex exhibits optimum catalytic performance among the studied configurations, effectively lowering the activation barrier and improving the reaction efficiency. Highlighting the significant role of size, edge conformation, and electronic structure in optimizing catalytic performance.

Using density functional theory, we extensively analysed the interaction between lithium-anchored graphene quantum dots (Li-GQDs) and various small molecules, including H2, N2, O2, CO, and CO2. Our findings confirm the stable integration of lithium atoms onto GQDs with both zigzag and armchair edge structures through exothermic interactions. This study evaluated the (i) electronic structural stability of Li-GQDs with different size and edge conformation, (ii) the binding strengths of small molecules with Li-GQDs, and (iii) changes in structural properties such as bond lengths and angles, alongside (iv) electronic properties and charge transfer mechanisms. For most molecules (H2, N2, CO, CO2), the binding energies suggest an intermediate between physisorption and chemisorption. The exception is a strong binding of O2 molecule onto Li centers of GQDs. Thus, Li decorated GQDs can function as catalysts for H2, N2, CO and CO2 molecules. The most potent catalyst is a 24 atom GQD with zig-zag edge which demonstrates the maximum bond red shift in the IR stretching frequencies and the bond lengths elongation of adsorbed H2, N2, CO and CO2 molecules. Increasing the size to 42 atoms does not enhance the catalytic potential of the GQD. These results are also corroborated by the lowest work function of 24-Li-GQD-zz complex. 24 atom GQD has the most common and widely synthesised GQD, known experimentally as coronene, demonstration of its better catalytic function when anchored with Li atom motivates the researches to evaluate this system experimentally.
